# Brief: Meg (PERSONA-011)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-30

# Forward-Looking Brief: Meg (Group Chat Relay) — March 30, 2026 Meeting

## Since Last Meeting

There are no new inter-meeting evidence events to report. The period between March 23 and March 30 has been quiet — no public statements, no documents released, no signals from board members or the administration about where things are heading.

For Meg, that silence has a practical meaning: whatever happened in her group chats this week was speculation, not news. She hasn't been able to add anything authoritative since the March 23 meeting ended at 11:15 PM without a vote. If anyone in her networks pushed back on something she said, she couldn't point to a new source to settle it — she's been holding at the March 23 record.

The biggest thing she's been sitting with: the board was handed two options (A and B) and explicitly did *not* vote. That's unusual for a meeting that ran five hours. The group chats almost certainly have theories about why, but Meg has nothing confirmable. She'll be walking into March 30 with no read on the room.

---

## Open Questions

Carrying these into March 30:

- **Did the board actually vote, and if so — which option?** That's the headline her group chats have been waiting for since March 23. If they vote tonight, this is the text she sends mid-meeting.
- **What exactly are Options A and B, and what does each one mean for specific schools and specific kids?** She understands both require Kayler to close, but she needs to know: does Option A mean Skillin kids go somewhere different than Option B? Which schools gain enrollment? Which lose staff?
- **Is it $7.2M or $8.4M?** Matthews put $8.4M on the record March 19. The official budget presentation March 23 used a different number. She doesn't have a clean answer if someone in her chat asks "wait, how big is the deficit exactly?"
- **Why does the pink slip count (72) not match the official position count (78)?** If someone asks her "wait, I thought they cut 78 jobs," what does she say? She needs a clean answer or she needs to know the board addressed it.
- **What's the actual status of the 32 teachers on the recall list?** Her friends want to know: if their kid's teacher got a pink slip, are they actually gone in September?
- **Are all 7 lunch aides really gone district-wide?** She mentioned this to her group chats after March 23. If it changes, she needs to be the one to correct it, not the one who posted the wrong thing.
- **What's the Title VI civil rights situation with Kayler?** Someone in her networks is going to ask about this. She needs to know: is there a legal opinion coming, and did anyone say that at the meeting?
- **Is there actually a vote tonight, or is this another five hours that ends without one?** That's the meta-question. If she tells people something is getting decided and it doesn't, she loses credibility.

---

## Agenda Implications

No agenda has been published for March 30. Based on how March 23 ended, here's what Meg should expect and prepare for:

**Most likely scenario: the board attempts to vote on elementary reconfiguration (Option A or B).** The March 23 meeting was structured as a presentation and deliberation — the five-hour runtime without a vote suggests the board wasn't ready, not that the issue was shelved. March 30 is the logical follow-on. If a vote happens, this is Meg's headline.

**What this means for Meg:**
The vote outcome — if it comes — will need to be texted accurately and fast. That means she needs to know *before* the meeting which option is which, so she's not scrambling to explain it at 10 PM when the chat explodes. Option A vs. Option B is abstract language. Her networks want to know: does Kayler close, and what happens to those kids? Which buildings get which grades?

**What she should listen for:**
- Any board member asking for *another delay* — if deliberation continues without a vote, that's still a story ("they still haven't decided") and she needs to say so clearly rather than leaving her chats hanging
- Any update on the Title VI legal review — whether the district has received a formal opinion, or whether that's still pending; this is the kind of detail that will matter in her chats the next morning when people have had time to Google it
- Any change to the $257/year household tax figure — if a board member cites a revised number, she needs to catch it; that's the figure she's been using as authoritative since March 23
- Any revised deficit figure — if the administration reconciles the $7.2M / $8.4M discrepancy officially, that's a correction she'll want to make in her networks
- Any clarification on the 72 vs. 78 position discrepancy — she should note the moment if it comes up, and the exact framing used

**Questions she should have ready if she can ask:**
- "When will the recall list be activated, and what triggers it?"
- "Which buildings specifically gain or lose enrollment under each option, and which grades?"
- "Has the district received a legal opinion on the Kayler Title VI question?"

---

## Watch For

- **The vote call itself** — board votes on school closures usually follow a formal motion and second before discussion closes. Listen for the motion language exactly: "I move to approve Option A" means something different than "I move to direct the superintendent to develop an implementation plan." She needs to know if the board is *deciding* or *directing further study*.
- **Board member alignment** — after a five-hour meeting that ended without a vote, at least some members were not ready. Watch for who speaks first, who asks for more time, and whether anyone signals a changed position from March 23. If a member who seemed hesitant on March 23 is now ready to vote, that's the signal that tonight ends differently.
- **DeAngelis's procedural management** — as chair, how she runs the meeting signals whether tonight is structured toward a vote. If she calls for a vote early in the discussion, that's different from an open-ended deliberation format. Watch whether she has a time limit framing at the top.
- **Any slide or document that's different from March 23** — if the administration comes back with revised materials (a new option, revised numbers, a legal memo on Title VI), that's news in its own right. Meg should screenshot the title slide of any new document shown during the stream.
- **The $257/year figure** — this is the number she's shared. If it's restated with a different methodology or changes, she needs to catch it in real time.
- **Audience size and tone** — after a five-hour meeting March 23, if public turnout on March 30 is significantly higher or lower, that's context worth one sentence in her summary ("packed room again" or "much quieter tonight").
- **Any mention of September 2026 implementation timeline** — if the board votes tonight but someone signals the September start is at risk, that's the detail parents in her networks need to know before they make summer plans.
- **The finance director's presence and demeanor** — Abigail Ketchem issued a warning at March 23 that FY28 will be structurally harder. If she presents again tonight or makes any statement about the current budget state, Meg should treat it as potentially material — the last time Ketchem spoke it changed the frame of the whole meeting.
- **Any mention of the 72 vs. 78 discrepancy** — if a board member or the administration clarifies this on the record, note the exact number used and who said it. This is the number her group chat contacts who followed the pink slips are going to ask about.
- **When the meeting ends relative to the vote** — if they vote at 8 PM, she can post a clean summary. If they're still deliberating at 10:30 PM, her networks are asleep and she'll be posting to a quiet chat. That timing affects how she structures her update.
